name: pyampute

metadata.name: pyampute
metadata.summary: Transformer for generating multivariate missingness in complete datasets

documentation: Amputation is the opposite of imputation; it is the creation of a missing data mask for complete datasets. Amputation is useful for evaluating the effect of missing values on the outcome of a statistical or machine learning model. `pyampute` is the first open-source Python library for data amputation. Our package is compatible with the scikit-learn-style fit and transform paradigm, which allows for seamless integration of amputation in a larger, more complex data processing pipeline.

modules.name: pyampute.utils
documentation: Utils mainly to write code agnostic to numpy or pandas.

functions.name: pyampute.utils.standardize_uppercase
documentation: Standardize string to upper case.

functions.name: pyampute.utils.enforce_numeric
documentation: Vars to enforce could be column names (str), indices (int), or a mask (bool).

modules.name: pyampute
documentation: Library with functions to explore, generate and solve missing data problems

modules.name: pyampute.exploration.md_patterns
documentation: Displays missing data patterns in incomplete datasets

classes.name: pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns

methods.name: pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns.get_patterns
documentation:
Extracts and visualizes missing data patterns in an incomplete dataset

# Parameters

X

: Dataset with missing values. `n` rows (samples) and `m` columns (features).

count_or_proportion

: Whether the number of rows should be specified as a count or a proportion.

show_plot

: Whether a plot should be displayed using `plt.show`.

# Returns

md_patterns

: `k` is the number of unique missing data patterns and `m` the number of dataset columns (features).
  The first row displays the data rows with no missing values and the last row gives column totals.
  The first column displays the count or proportion of rows that follow a pattern,
  the last column displays the number of missing values per pattern.

methods.name: pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns._calculate_patterns
documentation: Extracts all unique missing data patterns in an incomplete dataset and transforms into a pandas DataFrame

methods.name: pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns._make_plot
documentation: "Creates visualization of missing data patterns

Displays missing data patterns in incomplete datasets

Extracts all unique missing data patterns in an incomplete dataset and creates a visualization. `1` (red) and `0` (blue) refer to missing and observed values respectively.

# Parameters

None: currently no parameters available.

# Attributes

md_patterns

: `k` is the number of unique missing data patterns and `m` the number of dataset columns (features). `0` and `1` correspond to missing and observed values respectively. The first row displays the data rows with no missing values and the last row gives column totals. The first column displays the count or proportion of rows that follow a pattern and the last column displays the number of missing values per pattern.

# See also

{class}`~pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation` : Transformer for generating multivariate missingness in complete datasets

# Notes

This class is useful for investigating any structure in an incomplete dataset, and can help to understand possible reasons or solutions. We follow the logic of a comparable R-function, [mice::md_patterns].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> from pyampute.exploration.md_patterns import mdPatterns
>>> nhanes2 = pd.read_csv("data/nhanes2.csv")
>>> mdp = mdPatterns()
>>> patterns = mdp.get_patterns(nhanes2)
>>> print(patterns)
                        row_count  age  hyp  bmi  chl  n_missing_values
rows_no_missing                 13    1    1    1    1                 0
1                                3    1    1    1    0                 1
2                                1    1    1    0    1                 1
3                                1    1    0    0    1                 2
4                                7    1    0    0    0                 3
n_missing_values_per_col              0    8    9   10                27
```

[mice::md_patterns]: https://github.com/amices/mice

modules.name: pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests
documentation: Statistical hypothesis test for Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)

classes.name: pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests.MCARTest

methods.name: pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests.MCARTest.little_mcar_test
documentation:
Implementation of Little's MCAR test

Parameters
----------
X : Matrix of shape `(n, m)`
    Dataset with missing values. `n` rows (samples) and `m` columns (features).

Returns
-------
pvalue : float
    The p-value of a chi-square hypothesis test. Null hypothesis: data is Missing Completely At Random (MCAR). Alternative hypothesis: data is not MCAR.

methods.name: pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests.MCARTest.mcar_t_tests
documentation:
Performs t-tests for MCAR for each pair of features.

# Parameters

X

: Dataset with missing values. `n` rows (samples) and `m` columns (features).

# Returns

pvalues

: The p-values of t-tests for each pair of features. Null hypothesis for cell $pvalues[h,j]$: data in feature $h$ is Missing Completely At Random (MCAR) with respect to feature $j$ for all $h,j$ in ${1,2,...m}$. Diagonal values do not exist.

Statistical hypothesis test for Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)

Performs Little's MCAR test (see [Little, R.J.A. (1988)]). Null hypothesis: data is Missing Completely At Random (MCAR). Alternative hypothesis: data is not MCAR.

# Parameters

method

: Whether to perform a chi-square test on the entire dataset ("little") or separate t-tests for every combination of variables ("ttest").

# See also

{class}`~pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns` : Displays missing data patterns in incomplete datasets

{class}`~pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation` : Transformer for generating multivariate missingness in complete datasets

# Notes

We advise to use Little's MCAR test carefully. Rejecting the null hypothesis may not always mean that data is not MCAR, nor is accepting the null hypothesis a guarantee that data is MCAR. See [Schouten et al. (2021)] for a thorough discussion of missingness mechanisms.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> from pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests import MCARTest
>>> data_mcar = pd.read_table("data/missingdata_mcar.csv")
>>> mt = MCARTest(method="little")
>>> print(mt.little_mcar_test(data_mcar))
0.17365464213775494
```

[little, r.j.a. (1988)]: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/01621459.1988.10478722
[schouten et al. (2021)]: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/0049124118799376

modules.name: pyampute.ampute
documentation: Transformer for generating multivariate missingness in complete datasets

classes.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._shifted_probability_func
documentation:
Applies shifted custom function or sigmoid (with cutoff) to
standardized weighted sum scores to convert to probability.

String: sigmoid-
    - Right: Regular sigmoid pushes larger values to have high probability,
    - Left: To flip regular sigmoid across y axis, make input negative.
        This pushes smaller values to have high probability.
    - Mid: Values in the center of the score distribution have high probability.
    - Tail: Larger and smaller values have high probabiliy.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._binary_search
documentation:
Search for the appropriate shift/transformation to the weighted sum
scores in order to obtain the desired missingness proportion, before
passing through `self.score_to_probability_func`.
For instance, raw wss will mask 17% of samples in pattern $k$ but
you may want 40% missing.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._calculate_probabilities_from_wss
documentation: Returns the result of ``_shifted_probability_func``

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._choose_probabilities
documentation:
Assigns missingness probabilities to each sample (row) in the data
subset corresponding to pattern $k$ using the standardized wss.
These probabilities are later used to decide whether or not to apply
pattern $k$ to sample $i$.

When wss are all the same the mechanism is
: 1. MCAR: each case has an equal probability of becoming missing
     : (wss == 0)
  2. MAR with binary variables

Therefore we just use uniform probability of missing per var using
self.freqs. Alternatively, If there are not enough unique wss we
apply MCAR.

NOTE: The threshold is on unique WSS. The discrepancy between min
number of candidates requires and unique wss means that there are few
candidates and will trigger a warning, but if they're all unique wss,
then MCAR will NOT be applied.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._calculate_sumscores
documentation:
Creates a vector of weighted sum scores for all data rows (samples) in
the data subset corresponding to pattern $k$ by computing the
inner product of `self.weights` and the raw values of the samples in
that subset. This is later converted to a probability to be thresholded
on to decide whether or not to apply pattern $k$ to sample
$i$ in the data subset.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._get_default_pattern
documentation: Default pattern is a single pattern that works for any dataset.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._validate_indices_or_names
documentation: Validation of dict entries that are lists to be slotted into matrices.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._populate_pattern_array
documentation:
Fills an array of length $m$ (for each feature) with fill_value
wherever indicated by indices_or_names.

Column names will be mapped to their corresponding indices.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._pattern_dict_to_matrix_form
documentation: Converts the list of dictionaries into corresponding matrices and arrays. Each dict entry that's ArrayLike will transform into a matrix (`k`, `m`) e.g., weight array for pattern $k$ will define the row $k$ in the weights matrix. Each dict entry that's a single value will transform into an array of length $m$, e.g., freq for pattern $i$ will define $i$ th entry in freqs array.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._load_shift_lookup_table
documentation:
Read the lookup table csv from path.
Loads the table only once for the shift lookup
when computing missing probabilities from scores.
This is only useful for prespecified functions (e.g. sigmoid-right)

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._set_defaults
documentation:
Set defaults for args, assuming patterns has been initialized.
Most of the defaults rely on info from patterns.
Will adjust vars:
    change % to decimal, repeat for all patterns,
    standardize strings to uppercase force lists to np arrays, etc.)

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._validate_args
documentation:
Validates remainined constructor args after having set defaults.
Only makes assertions, assuming everything is initialized.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation._validate_data
documentation:
Validate passed data for transform.
Will modify the dataset to comply if possible, while giving warnings.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation.fit
documentation:
Fits amputer on complete data X.

Validates input data and parameter settings.

Parameters
----------
X : Matrix
    Matrix of shape `(n, m)`.
    Complete input data, where `n` is the number of data rows (samples)
    and `m` is the number of features (column, variables). Data cannot
    contain missing values and should be numeric, or will be forced to
    be numeric.

y : ArrayLike
    Ignored. 
    Not used, present here for consistency.

methods.name: pyampute.ampute.MultivariateAmputation.transform
documentation:
Masks data according to the desired pattern and returns the incomplete data X.

Parameters
----------        
X : Matrix
    Matrix of shape `(n, m)`
    Complete input data, where `n` is the number of data rows (samples) and `m` is the number of features (column, variables).
    Data cannot contain missing values and should be numeric,
    or will be forced to be numeric.

y : ArrayLike
    Ignored. 
    Not used, present here for consistency.

Returns
-------
X_incomplete : Matrix
    Matrix of shape `(n, m)`.
    Incomplete data masked according to parameters.

Generating multivariate missingness patterns in complete datasets

- `n` = number of samples.
- `m` = number of features/variables.
- `k` = number of patterns.

Amputation is the opposite of imputation: the generation of missing values in complete datasets.
This is useful for evaluating the effect of missing values in your model, possibly in a larger pipeline in combination with various imputation methods, estimators and evaluation metrics.

We provide [several examples] and [an extensive blogpost] to explain in more detail how certain parameter choices affect the generated missingness.

Our class is compatible with the scikit-learn-style `fit` and `transform` paradigm and can be used in a scikit-learn `Pipeline` ([see this example]).

# Parameters

prop

: Proportion of incomplete data rows as a decimal or percent.

patterns

: List of `k` dictionaries. Each dictionary has the following key-value pairs:

  > **incomplete_vars** (Union\[ArrayLike[int], ArrayLike[str]\]) --
  >
  > : Indicates which variables that should be amputed.
  >   List of int for indices of variables, list of str for column names of variables.
  >   `observed_vars` is the complement of `incomplete_vars`.
  >
  > **weights** (Union\[ArrayLike[float], Dict[int, float], Dict[str, float]\], default: all 0s (MCAR) or `observed_vars` weight 1 (MAR) or `incomplete_vars` weight 1 (MNAR)) --
  >
  > : Specifies the (relative) size of effect of each specified var on missing vars.
  >   If using an array, you must specify all *m* weights.
  >   If using a dictionary, the keys are either indices of vars or column names; unspecified vars will be assumed to have a weight of 0.
  >   Negative values have a decrease effect, 0s indicate no role in missingness, and positive values have an increase effect.
  >   The weighted score for sample `i` in pattern `k` is the inner product of the `weights` and `sample[i]`.
  >   *Note:* weights are required to be defined if the corresponding mechanism is MAR+MNAR.
  >
  > **mechanism** (`str, {MAR, MCAR, MNAR, MAR+MNAR}`) --
  >
  > : Case insensitive. MNAR+MAR is only possible by passing a custom weight array.
  >
  > **freq** (*float [0,1], default: all patterns with equal frequency (1/k)*) --
  >
  > : Relative occurence of a pattern with respect to other patterns.
  >   All frequencies across `k` dicts/patterns must sum to 1.
  >   Either specify for all patterns, or none for the default.
  >   For example (`k` = 3 patterns), `freq := [0.4, 0.4, 0.2]`
  >   means that of all rows with missing values, 40% should have pattern 1, 40% pattern 2. and 20% pattern 3.
  >
  > **score_to_probability_func** (`Union[str, Callable[ArrayLike[floats] -> ArrayLike[floats]]], {"sigmoid-right", "sigmoid-left", "sigmoid-mid", "sigmoid-tail", Callable}`) --
  >
  > : Converts standardized weighted scores for each data row (in a data subset corresponding to pattern k) to probability of missingness.
  >   Choosing one of the sigmoid options (case insensitive) applies sigmoid function with a logit cutoff per pattern.
  >   The simgoid functions dictate that a [high, low, average, extreme] score (respectively) has a high probability of amputation.
  >   The sigmoid functions will be shifted to ensure correct joint missingness probabilities.
  >   Custom functions must accept arrays with values `(-inf, inf)` and output values `[0,1]`.
  >   We will *not* shift custom functions, refer to {ref}`sphx_glr_auto_examples_plot_custom_probability_function.py` for more.

std

: Whether or not to standardize data before computing weighted scores.
  Standardization ensures that weights can be interpreted relative to each other.
  Do not standardize if train and test split is done after amputation (prevent leaking).

verbose: bool, default

: Toggle on to see INFO level logging information.

seed: int, optional

: If you want reproducible results during amputation, set an integer seed.
  If you don't set it, a random number will be produced every time.

lower_range

: Lower limit in range when searching for horizontal shift of `score_to_probability_func`.

upper_range

: Upper limit in range when searching for horizontal shift of `score_to_probability_func`.

max_dif_with_target

: The allowable error between the desired percent of missing data (`prop`)
  and calculated joint missingness probability.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations for binary search when searching for horizontal shift of `score_to_probability_func`.

# Attributes

DEFAULT_PATTERN: Dict[str, Any]

: If `patterns` are not passed, the default is the following:

  ```python
  {
      "incomplete_vars": random 50% of vars,
      "mechanism": "MAR",
      "freq": 1
      "score-to-prob": "sigmoid-right"

  }
  ```

DEFAULTS

: Default values used, especially if values are not passed for parameters in certain patterns (not to be confused with patterns not being specified at all).

# See also

{class}`~pyampute.exploration.md_patterns.mdPatterns` : Displays missing data patterns in incomplete datasets

{class}`~pyampute.exploration.mcar_statistical_tests.MCARTest` : Statistical hypothesis test for Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)

# Notes

The methodology for multivariate amputation has been proposed by [Schouten et al. (2018)]. For a more thorough understanding of how the input parameters can be used, read [this blogpost]. It may be good to know that multivariate amputation is implemented in an R-function as well; [mice::ampute].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from pyampute.ampute import MultivariateAmputation
>>> n = 1000
>>> m = 10
>>> X_compl = np.random.randn(n,m)
>>> ma = MultivariateAmputation()
>>> X_incompl = ma.fit_transform(X_compl)
```

[an extensive blogpost]: https://rianneschouten.github.io/pyampute/build/html/mapping.html
[mice::ampute]: https://rianneschouten.github.io/mice_ampute/vignette/ampute.html
[schouten et al. (2018)]: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00949655.2018.1491577
[see this example]: https://rianneschouten.github.io/pyampute/build/html/auto_examples/plot_simulation_pipeline.html
[several examples]: https://rianneschouten.github.io/pyampute/build/html/examples.html
[this blogpost]: https://rianneschouten.github.io/pyampute/build/html/mapping.html
[transformermixin]: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.base.TransformerMixin.html
